notes.note: Cicero calls the assassinated Caesar a "king" (rex), the charge the Liberators used to justify the killing; his bitter point is that Caesar is dead yet Rome is still not free.
The people of Buthrotum in Epirus, near Atticus's estate; their town faced confiscation for back taxes, and Cicero spent much of 44 BC trying to protect them on Atticus's behalf.

Income-bearing property at Puteoli that Cicero had inherited from the banker Marcus Cluvius; despite a structural collapse it was still yielding rent, here reckoned at around a hundred thousand sesterces.

The nineteen-year-old Gaius Octavius (the future Augustus), Caesar's testamentary heir, newly landed in Italy; he had taken up residence in his stepfather Philippus's villa next to Cicero's Puteoli estate and was assiduously courting Cicero.

notes.anchor: the villa of Philippus right next door
notes.note: L. Marcius Philippus, consul 56 BC and Octavian's stepfather, owned a seaside villa adjacent to Cicero's; his refusal later to greet the youth as "Caesar" is noted in 14.12.
